payload.instructions:
## Skills
These skills are discovered at startup from multiple local sources. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- Discovery: Available skills are listed in project docs and may also appear in a runtime "## Skills" section (name + description + file path). These are the sources of truth; skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Description as trigger: The YAML `description` in `SKILL.md` is the primary trigger signal; rely on it to decide applicability. If unsure, ask a brief clarification before proceeding.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deeply nested references; prefer one-hop files explicitly linked from `SKILL.md`.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.

# AGENTS.md instructions for /Users/duhl/git/anedot

<INSTRUCTIONS>
## Skills
These skills are discovered at startup from multiple local sources. Each entry includes a name, description, and file path so you can open the source for full instructions.
- skill-creator: Guide for creating effective skills. This skill should be used when users want to create a new skill (or update an existing skill) that extends Codex's capabilities with specialized knowledge, workflows, or tool integrations. (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-creator/SKILL.md)
- skill-installer: Install Codex skills into $CODEX_HOME/skills from a curated list or a GitHub repo path. Use when a user asks to list installable skills, install a curated skill, or install a skill from another repo (including private repos). (file: /Users/duhl/.codex/skills/.system/skill-installer/SKILL.md)
- Discovery: Available skills are listed in project docs and may also appear in a runtime "## Skills" section (name + description + file path). These are the sources of truth; skill bodies live on disk at the listed paths.
- Trigger rules: If the user names a skill (with `$SkillName` or plain text) OR the task clearly matches a skill's description, you must use that skill for that turn. Multiple mentions mean use them all. Do not carry skills across turns unless re-mentioned.
- Missing/blocked: If a named skill isn't in the list or the path can't be read, say so briefly and continue with the best fallback.
- How to use a skill (progressive disclosure):
  1) After deciding to use a skill, open its `SKILL.md`. Read only enough to follow the workflow.
  2) If `SKILL.md` points to extra folders such as `references/`, load only the specific files needed for the request; don't bulk-load everything.
  3) If `scripts/` exist, prefer running or patching them instead of retyping large code blocks.
  4) If `assets/` or templates exist, reuse them instead of recreating from scratch.
- Description as trigger: The YAML `description` in `SKILL.md` is the primary trigger signal; rely on it to decide applicability. If unsure, ask a brief clarification before proceeding.
- Coordination and sequencing:
  - If multiple skills apply, choose the minimal set that covers the request and state the order you'll use them.
  - Announce which skill(s) you're using and why (one short line). If you skip an obvious skill, say why.
- Context hygiene:
  - Keep context small: summarize long sections instead of pasting them; only load extra files when needed.
  - Avoid deeply nested references; prefer one-hop files explicitly linked from `SKILL.md`.
  - When variants exist (frameworks, providers, domains), pick only the relevant reference file(s) and note that choice.
- Safety and fallback: If a skill can't be applied cleanly (missing files, unclear instructions), state the issue, pick the next-best approach, and continue.
</INSTRUCTIONS>
I need a good PR message for the latest commit

--- a/lib/validation_helper.rb

@@ -78,7 +78,7 @@ module ValidationHelper

-  self.facebook_pixel_id_regex = /\A[0-9]{15,16}\z/
+  self.facebook_pixel_id_regex = /\A[0-9]{15,17}\z/
   self.facebook_pixel_id_format = {
     with: facebook_pixel_id_regex,
     message: facebook_pixel_id_bad_message
diff --git a/spec/lib/validation_helper_spec.rb b/spec/lib/validation_helper_spec.rb
index 4c5da5bb51..730e8114b7 100644
--- a/spec/lib/validation_helper_spec.rb
+++ b/spec/lib/validation_helper_spec.rb
@@ -226,7 +226,7 @@
 
     it "does not match invalid values" do
       expect(regex).not_to match("4358234322")
-      expect(regex).not_to match("14351346342623664")
+      expect(regex).not_to match("143513463426236648")
       expect(regex).not_to match("DSF78923K3P3")
       expect(regex).not_to match("142324&*)07")

diff --git a/spec/models/action_page_general_configuration_spec.rb b/spec/models/action_page_general_configuration_spec.rb

--- a/spec/models/action_page_general_configuration_spec.rb

       end
     end
 
-    context "with facebook pixel ID that is beyond 16 chars" do
+    context "with facebook pixel ID that is beyond 17 chars" do
       it "is not valid" do
         account = create(:account, :with_defaults)
         config = build(
           :action_page_general_configuration,
-          facebook_pixel: "a" * 17, account:
+          facebook_pixel: "a" * 18, account:
         )
 
         expect(config.valid?).to be(false)
         expect(config.errors.full_messages).to include(
-          "Facebook pixel is too long (maximum is 16 characters)"
+          "Facebook pixel is too long (maximum is 17 characters)"
         )
       end
     end

payload.message:
PR message you can use:

Title:
Increase Facebook Pixel ID max length to 17

Body:
- raise Facebook Pixel ID validations to 17 chars across models/config
- widen regex to allow 15–17 digits
- update DB schema + migration for pixel ID columns
- adjust specs for new length limits

Want me to tailor it to your team’s PR template (e.g., Jira/Linear links, risk, testing)?

Title:
Increase Facebook Pixel ID max length to 17

Body:
- raise Facebook Pixel ID validations to 17 chars across models/config
- widen regex to allow 15–17 digits
- update DB schema + migration for pixel ID columns

Want me to tailor it to your team’s PR template (e.g., Jira/Linear links, risk, testing)?
